evidence_quotes.quote: The Digital Fairness Act would flourish with the addition to protection for cultural media such as but not limited to video games, lets take a fairly recent case: The Crew.
we've seen some minor improvements regarding rare instances where monetization laws and gambling laws have been put into effect against loot boxes

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act would flourish with the addition to protection for cultural media such as but not limited to video games, lets take a fairly recent case: The Crew. Released in 2014 was by all means an online only game upon it's debut however as discovered by the community the game did have a locked down offline mode this would not be an issue if we look back at similar situations with Need for Speed World where the community were able to make their own servers where you could play the game. However recently from these past two decades companies have step by step introduced less consumer friendly practices which has resulted in what we now are suffering the consequences of, Need for Speed world shutting meant you could not play the official online servers but you could still download the game unlike our most recent case The Crew that was not only delisted but taken away from the library of the players that did have that game which means you can not even download the game. All of this would not be a problem if the companies knew that either existing or new consumer laws would apply to them as it does other trades, we've seen some minor improvements regarding rare instances where monetization laws and gambling laws have been put into effect against loot boxes however these cases are too far in between where companies do not care enough about the potential economic consequences due to the rare chance it ever reaches that far. This is not solely a problem for AAA PC/Console and arguably an even more of a problem concerning mobile applications where they generate millions in revenue from addiction akin to gambling, then there is the issue where payment processors such as mastercard/visa are able to be pressured into essentially distrupting business and ruling what we as consumers are allowed to purchase. My overall point is this: Consumer laws and Consumer rights should apply even for this type of "service/business" and these laws and rules should reflect what is needed to prevent abuse and hostility between consumers and companies, We may be able to play and purchase games from over 20 years ago (example: Command and Conquers) But not games made after that? The business practice of today is non-acceptable and there is a need for digital preservation through legal means, piracy has been in many ways the only "means of preservation" for countless work of arts ranging from music to games, most notoriously of them all is Nintendo, piracy had been in a decline after not only the court ruling against the pirate bay but also due to how companies managed to make products not much of a hassle to handle through legitimate means. However if these companies continue to go un-noticed with their practices, there is a guaranteed risk of piracy becoming popular and perhaps even mainstream again. Therefore I join the many voices calling for the videogames industry to receive attention under this initiative, as it has operated with insufficient accountability for far too long. Thank you for the opportunity to provide input and I look forward to seeing the positive impact of this initiative.
